coredata.dc:title: Preliminary report on therapeutic potential of coal-derived carbon quantum dots against SARS-CoV-2 virus
coredata.dc:creator: Saikia, Binoy K.
Roy, Kallol
Konwar, Rituraj
coredata.dc:description: Due to the pandemic of COVID-19 and subsequent emerging of new mutant strains, there has been a worldwide hunt for therapeutic and protective agents for its inhibition. In this short communication, for the first time, we report the coal-derived carbon quantum dot (CQD) for the possible therapeutic application against SARS-CoV-2. The synthesized C1-CQD is observed to be safe towards the normal cell line at highest dose, while effectively inhibiting growth of SARS-CoV2 (>95%) with IC50 value of 5.469 μg/mL. Moreover, C1-CQD showed activity against SARS-CoV-2 infection which is comparable to known inhibitory antiviral drug i.e., Remdesivir. These novel findings indicate that coal-based CQDs have highly potent anti-viral activity and could be investigated further for developing cheap and safer alternative therapeutic strategies for inhibition of SARS-CoV-2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Coal
SARS-CoV-2
COVID-19
Anti-viral
Toxicity
Viral replication

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: High resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic analysis of C1-CQDs
Antiviral activity of C1-CQDs against SARS-CoV-2 infection

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This article is made available under the Elsevier license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are grateful to the Director (CSIR-NEIST) for his keen interest to this research work. Authors are also thankful to CSIR (OLP-2077) for the funding and Kallol Roy is thankful to UGC for his fellowship. We also acknowledge Monikankana for providing the sample and ILS, Bhubaneswar for the antiviral testing. The constructive comments received from the anonymous reviewers and Lead Editor (Dr Jasmine Tomar) are thankfully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (insert SAED pattern indicates the presence of (004), (101), and (100) planes of graphitized carbon) of C1-CQDs at 20 nm (a), corresponding HRTEM image at 10 nm (b), and particle size distribution between 4 and 16 nm sizes with average size 7 nm (c).
High resolution XPS survey spectrum (a) and deconvoluted C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) spectra analysis of C1-CQDs.
Cytotoxicity of C1-CQD on Vero E6 (a) and HEK-293 (b) cells using MTT assay.
ROS generation analysis of C1-CQDs at 100 and 200 μg/mL in HEK-293 cells.
Dose dependent anti-viral activity of coal-derived C1-CQD against SARS-CoV-2 infection.
Anti-viral activity of C1-CQD and FDA approved drug, Remdesivir at dose of 100 μg/mL.

head.ce:title: Preliminary report on therapeutic potential of coal-derived carbon quantum dots against SARS-CoV-2 virus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Coal & Energy Division (C&E), CSIR-North East Institute of Science & Technology, Jorhat, 785006, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-North East Institute of Science & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the pandemic of COVID-19 and subsequent emerging of new mutant strains, there has been a worldwide hunt for therapeutic and protective agents for its inhibition. In this short communication, for the first time, we report the coal-derived carbon quantum dot (CQD) for the possible therapeutic application against SARS-CoV-2. The synthesized C1-CQD is observed to be safe towards the normal cell line at highest dose, while effectively inhibiting growth of SARS-CoV2 (>95%) with IC50 value of 5.469 μg/mL. Moreover, C1-CQD showed activity against SARS-CoV-2 infection which is comparable to known inhibitory antiviral drug i.e., Remdesivir. These novel findings indicate that coal-based CQDs have highly potent anti-viral activity and could be investigated further for developing cheap and safer alternative therapeutic strategies for inhibition of SARS-CoV-2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The eradication and prevention of viral disease is a major challenge because of high spreading in nature and alternation of the genetic makeup. Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2) is one of the deadliest types of coronaviruses was first detected in the city of Wuhan, in the province of Hubei (China). On March 11th 2020, World Health Organization (WHO) declared the disease spread by virus is called COVID-19 and the outbreaks as COVID-19 pandemic (Hu et al., 2021). According to the WHO list, around 761,769,759 confirmed cases of COVID-19 including 6,784,181 deaths worldwide as of September 20, 2023 (https://covid19.who.int). To date, the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) have fully approved four drugs, namely Veklury (Remdesivir), Paxlovid (nirmatrelvir and ritonavir), Actemra (Tocilizumab), and Olumiant (baricitinib) for patient suffering from SARS-CoV-2 infection (https://www.fda.gov/consumers/consumer-updates/know-your-treatment-options-covid-19).

Carbon nanomaterials have provided as promising and efficient platforms to modulate the viral infection cycle and its prevention. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are newly emerging nanomaterial can be considered as a promising solution in the pursuit of an effective therapeutic strategy against COVID-19 due to unique morphological properties and easily pass through the blood brain barrier. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs, C-dots or CDs) are defined by carbon based zero dimensional (0D) inorganic nanoparticle with sizes below 10 nm which were accidently found during the purification of single walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) through preparative electrophoresis method and can be synthesized by top down (arc discharge, acidic oxidation, and laser ablation) and bottom up approach (combustion/thermal routes, electrochemistry method microwave pyrolysis, and hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis) (Saikia et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019). CQDs are gaining special interest owing to water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and inherent fluorescent properties.

CQDs have been widely studied in the multidisciplinary fields such as in bioimaging, optronics, drug delivery, biosensors, and anti-microbial applications (Wu et al., 2022; Rabe et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018; Bagheri et al., 2018). CQDs are also reported to exhibit anti-viral activity against wide range of viruses such as HSV-1, ZIKV, DENV, and NoV based on the masking and inhibition of viral entry receptor and membrane disruption (Barras et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2017). It has been reported that during infection CQD strigger antioxidant defense system and activation of type I interferon IFN-α, IFN-β, and ISGs proteins expression to prevent viral replication and proliferation process (Liu et al., 2017; Tong et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Aung et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2019). Recent reviews have also discussed the potential of CQDs to combat SARS-CoV-2 virus infection (Kotta et al., 2020). However, the application of coal-derived CQDs as an antiviral drug, more specifically against SARS-CoV-2 virus has not been investigated so far as per our best of literature survey. It was reported that the coal-derived CQDs is smaller in size, have good aqueous solubility and biocompatibility, and the presence of various surface functional groups (e.g., -OH and -COOH) could interact with viral surface to inhibit viral infection (Saikia et al., 2022). Therefore, in the present study, we have chosen the CQDs derived from sub-bituminous Indian coal to investigate their anti-viral role against SARS-CoV-2 virus infection for the first time. We also compared anti-viral activity of C1-CQD with FDA approved anti-viral drug, Remdesivir and examined its toxicity towards normal cells for its possible future utility as new therapeutic strategy.

EZcount™ MTT cell assay kit (Himedia, Mumbai, India), DMEM (Himedia) media with 10% FBS, Gentamycin, Penicillin-streptomycin, Trypsin, cDNA first strand synthesis kit (Takara Bio, India), Mesagreen SYBR Green-No ROX, (Eurogentec, Belgium), TANBead Maelstrom™ 4800 automated RNA extraction platform (Taiwan Advanced Nanotech). Virus used for this study was originally isolated, adapted and characterized from the swab sample of the patient as SARS-CoV-2 strain (Accession no. EPI_ISL_1,196,305) as reported previously (Singh et al., 2022). Vero E6 cells (Monkey kidney epithelial cell line) stock available and maintained in the laboratory were used to transfect with the virus strain for the experiments. HEK-293 (Human embryonic kidney cell line) stock available in the laboratory.

The C1-CQDs were synthesized from low quality subbituminous coal samples of North-Eastern India. The synthesis steps of inexpensive C1-CQDs from coal were followed as reported previously (Saikia et al., 2019, 2020; Boruah et al., 2020; Das et al., 2019). Briefly, 20 g of powdered sub-bituminous coal sample was mixed with 200 mL of hydrogen peroxide (30%) in an ice-cold condition followed by ultrasonicate at 40 kHz for 5–6 h. The resulting solution was neutralized with solution of ammonium hydroxide (25%) and filtered with 0.22-μm filter paper. Finally, purified by using ultrafiltration and resulting CQDs was kept in 4 °C freeze.

The TEM/HRTEM (JEOL, JEM-2100) was performed to evaluate the morphology and microstructure/nanostructure of the synthesized coal-derived C1-CQDs. For TEM analysis, the sample was prepared by placing two drops of C1-CQDs suspension on carbon-coated copper grids (300 μm) and allow it to dry prior to analysis. The TEM images were further analyzed by using Image J software (software version 1.47). X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to determine surface structure and nature of chemical bonding using X-Ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB Xi +). The liquid C1-CQDs sample was coated in small glass and over dried before analysis.

The cytotoxicity test of C1-CQDs sample by MTT assay was performed as per the protocol given elsewhere (https://www.ils.res.in/biovalidation-service/). In a brief, Vero E6 cell line was cultured and seeded in 96 well plates at 80% confluency followed by incubation with C1-CQDs at their different concentrations of 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL for 24 h. After 24 h of post treatment, each well was supplemented of MTT solution to determine toxicity of sample and the percentage (%) of cell viability was calculated in compared to vehicle control.

Similarly, we have tested the cytotoxic effects of C1-CQDs in HEK-293 cell line using MTT assay as reported earlier (Saini et al., 2017). Briefly, 5 x 103 HEK-293 cells were seeded in 96 well plates and incubated for 24 h. Thereafter, cells were treated with C1-CQDs at their various concentration of 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL in 200 μl for further 24 h. At the end of treatment, MTT was added in each well and incubated for 2 h. After that, 100 μl DMSO was added to each well and absorbance was taken at 540 nm in microplate reader (Bio Tek, India).

The effects of C1-CQDs on intracellular ROS generation in HEK-293 cell lines was analyzed using 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) fluorescent dye. In a brief, 2 x 105 HEK-293 cells were seeded in 24 well culture plate and allow to grow for 24 h. Cells were treated with 100 and 200 μg/mL concentration of C1-CQDs for 24 h followed by addition of 500 μl DCFH-DA (10 μM) for 30 min under dark condition. After being washed with PBS cells were incubated with radioimmunoprecipitation assay (RIPA) buffer for 5 min at 4 °C. Thereafter cells were harvested by centrifugation at 21,130×g for 10 min at 4 °C and intracellular ROS generation was analyzed at excitation and emission wavelengths of 485 and 530 nm using fluorescence microplate reader (Bio Tek, Cytation 5).

To determine the antiviral activity of C1-CQDs, 80% confluent Vero cells were seeded in 96 well plates before infection with SARS-CoV-2 virus. Thereafter, Vero cells were infected with SARS-CoV-2 isolate at a multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 0.1 for 2 h and exposed to five different (5–100 μg/mL) concentrations C1-CQDs for 22 h at 37 °C CO2 incubator. Subsequently 22 h post-infection (hpi) the supernatant was collected for RNA extraction and subjected to viral RNA isolation followed by quantitative real time PCR (qRT-PCR) for determining the SARS-CoV-2 viral load in the cells and culture supernatants (released virus particles). Additionally, the anti-viral activity of C1-CQD was also checked at highest dose of 100 μg/mL in comparison to known inhibitory anti-viral FDA approved drug, Remdesivir.

qRT-PCR was performed using Viral spike; nucleocapsid (Nucleocapsid Forward Primer (NCFP): GTAACACAAGCTTTCGGCAG and Nucleocapsid Reverse Primer (NCRP): GTGTGACTTCCATGCCAATG) and ORF1a specific probe: (50-FAM-CAGGTGGAACCTCATCAG GAGATGC-BHQ-30) were used for determining the viral load in both treated and untreated samples (Kode et al., 2023; Singh et al., 2022). Percentage reduction of virions in cells and culture supernatants was plotted in comparison to vehicle controls.

The TEM/HRTEM images displayed the detailed morphology and microstructure/nanostructure of C1-CQDs derived from the feed coal sample. Fig. 1 (a, b) showed the homogenous spherical shape of CQDs particle distribution without aggregation.

It was observed that the particle diameter ranges from 4 to 16 nm with average diameter of 7 nm (Fig. 1c). The crystallinity and plane of C1-CQDs were determined using HRTEM in accordance with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. The SAED patterns (see inset of Fig. 1 a) showed diffused rings signifies the amorphous or partially crystalline in nature and (100), (101), and (004) planes indicate the presence of graphitized carbon in the sample (Saikia et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: High resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic analysis of C1-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical compositions of C1-CQDs were analyzed by X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) in order to evaluate their chemical and bonding environment. The survey scan XPS spectra in Fig. 2 (a) depicts that C1-CQDs mainly showed three distinct peaks at binding energies of 284, 400, and 531 eV corresponding to the presence of C, N, and O atoms. The deconvoluted C1S spectra in Fig. 2 (b) depicts two peaks at binding energies of 283.2 and 287.1 eV attributed to C–C sp2 and O–C=O bonds, respectively. Deconvoluted spectra of N1S at binding energies 398 and 399.9 eV attributed to the presence of C–N and Pyrrolic N and while O1S deconvoluted spectra suggested that the occurrence of organic C–O bond at binding energies of 530.2 eV (Fig. 2 c & d). It can be summarized that the coal derived C1-CQDs possess oxygen containing functional groups with sp2 structural units. Additionally, surface oxygen functional groups lead to high aqueous solubility and fluorescent making it advantageous in biological applications.

In vitro cytotoxicity of C1-CQDs against Vero E6 and HEK-293 cell lines were performed via MTT assay. For that, Vero and HEK-293 cells were co-incubated with C1-CQDs from low to highest concentration of 100 μg/mL and 200 μg/mL respectively for 24 h. It was observed that even at highest dose percentage (%) of cell viability was found to be 95% for Vero cells and 94% for HEK-293 cells, confirming no sign of toxicity (Fig. 3 ).

The ROS generation analysis using DCFH-DA staining in HEK-293 cell lines revealed that C1-CQDs at dose of 100 and 200 μg/mL are not capable in generating ROS. This study demonstrated that the anti-viral mechanism of coal derived C1-CQDs against SARS-CoV-2 probably not due to ROS mediated pathway (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antiviral activity of C1-CQDs against SARS-CoV-2 infection
ce:section.ce:para: As several previous reports demonstrated remarkable anti-viral activity of quantum dots against different viruses utilizing diverse mechanisms of action, we carried out screening of anti-SARS-CoV-2 activity of our as-synthesized coal-based C1-CQDs. The antiviral activity of C1-CQDs against SARS-CoV-2 viral infection was evaluated on infection of Vero cell with that virus and collected supernatants at 22 hpi. It was observed that with the increasing concentration of the anti-viral activity of C1-CQD significant increased with % reduction of 99.3% at 100 μg/mL as compared to control. Moreover, the IC50 of the coal-derived C1-CQDs were against SARS-CoV-2 was determined to be 5.469 μg/mL (Table 1 ).

The Ct value of C1-CQD obtained against SARS-CoV-2 by qRT-PCR was found to be 28.350 in comparison to infected only (0.1 MOI) Ct value of 22.168. It also noteworthy to mention that C1-CQDs exhibited a strong activity to SARS-CoV-2 virus similar to FDA approved antiviral drug i.e., Remdesivir which has the mean Ct value of 29.313 (Table 2 ). Here, we would like to state that the observations of this study were derived from single independent experimentation and needed further investigation.

It is known that the first event of virus-cell interactions involves the attachment and adsorption of viral particles host cell receptors, penetration and hijack host cell machinery followed by release of virions from infected host cells to spread neighbour cells. Thus, anti-viral drugs can disrupt any or several of these critical steps of involved in viral infection and propagation. Recent reports elaborately discussed the potency of carbon-based nanomaterial in prevention and detection of wide range of viruses including SARS-CoV-2 viral infection (Ahmadi et al., 2023; Xue et al., 2022). The major interest of carbon quantum dot application against virus is rapidly increasing owing to its biocompatibility, cell permeability, and smaller (≤10 nm) size which can be useful in the inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 virus (Ahmadi et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2020). The as-synthesized coal-derived C1-CQDs with functional groups (e.g., -OH and -COOH) showing significant antiviral activity comparable to that of standard drug, Remdesivir suggest that it certainly act on certain steps of viral infection as discussed above. In order to presume the underlying inhibitory mechanism of C1-CQD, it may be plausible that there may be interaction of hydrophilic functional groups present in C1-CQD with viral receptors thereby preventing its entry via binding to SARS-CoV-2 spike protein or abrogate replication-budding step through binding to nucleocapsid proteins. However, further detailed study will be required to decipher the exact mechanism behind this SARS-CoV-2 inhibitory activity of C1-CQD. Previously it was reported that the curcumin based cationic carbon dot (CCM-CD) can effectively inhibit SARS-CoV-2 virus model, porcine epidemic diarrhea virus (PEDV) entry, production of the negative strand of RNA, and budding. The inhibitory mechanism is based on the enhancement of proinflammatory cytokines expression and ROS accumulation in the infected host cell (Ting et al., 2018; Sanchez de Araujo and Ferreira, 2021). Similarly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with boronic acid and 4-aminophenylboronic acid have been reported to inhibit HCoV-229E entry and replication via direct interaction of functional group of CQDs with HCoV-229E S protein or with an entry factor and activation of proinflammatory cytokines (Loczechin et al., 2019). Molecular docking and molecular dynamics stimulation studies showed that OH-CQD (80%) and NH2–OH-CQD (85%) were more functional than COOH-CQD and amide-CQD, in terms of blocking RBD of SARS-CoV-2 spike glycoprotein (Ramezani et al., 2021). In a recent study, the triazole functionalized heteroatom co-doped carbon quantum dots (TFH-CQDs) combat human coronavirus SARSCoV-2 infection using series of bio isosteres. The result showed that TFH-CQDs perturb the viral entry through interaction viral entry receptor DPP4 as well as block 3CL-pro protein and helicase enzymes to inhibit viral replications (Garg et al., 2020). It was reported that masking of host cell ACE2 receptors or blockage of receptor-binding domains (RBD) of SARS-CoV-2 can be a key strategy to impede its infection (Shukla et al., 2021.). Further extending this, later other group had conceptualized that fluorescent CQD decorated with both angiotensin II could compete with the ACE2 receptor to prevent its binding to cell surface receptor (Bag and Sinha, 2020). Here, further mechanistic studies of anti-viral activity of C1-CQD can explain how such undecorated CQDs can exert anti-viral activity against viruses like SARS-CoV-2.

In conclusion, C1-CQDs derived from subbituminous Indian coal shows promising anti-viral activity against SARS-CoV-2 infection. Cytotoxicity study of C1-CQDS in Vero cells line showed it to be safe with 95% cell viability at highest dose of 100 μg/mL. C1-CQD exhibited significant anti-viral activity towards SARS-CoV-2 with 98.2% inhibition which is comparable to FDA approved drug, Remdesivir. The current study opens up a new pathway of using novel coal-based inexpensive CQDs for possible therapeutic applications against SARS-CoV-2. More detailed in vitro and in vivo studies are necessary to understand the mechanistic pathways and therapeutic effects of CQDs.

Binoy K. Saikia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Kallol Roy: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Rituraj Konwar: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the Director (CSIR-NEIST) for his keen interest to this research work. Authors are also thankful to CSIR (OLP-2077) for the funding and Kallol Roy is thankful to UGC for his fellowship. We also acknowledge Monikankana for providing the sample and ILS, Bhubaneswar for the antiviral testing. The constructive comments received from the anonymous reviewers and Lead Editor (Dr Jasmine Tomar) are thankfully acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exploring the potential of carbon dots to combat COVID-19
